Hard comparison.

Based on my pre-existing knowledge of the ecosystem and the project descriptions, Vyper is constrained to a more focused use case as smart contract compiler, where-as foundry is a broader toolset for building full smart contract projects.  Foundry uses Solidity which has a much broader user base.  Looking at github star and fork metrics lines up with the assessment that Foundry is more important, with fork count suggesting foundry being 2.19x more valuable than vyper and star count suggesting foundry bing 1.73x more valuable than vyper.  Using "github topics" (https://github.com/topics/), foundry has 998 repositories vs vyper's 199 repositories, suggesting foundry is 5.01x more valuable.  Looking at the solidity ecosystem, which has 19,411 repositories on github, this further re-inforces foundry being more important.  Diversity of tools is valuable in our ecosystem, however, my personal opinion is that Vyper does not represent healthy diversity as I'm not confident that it would be a responsiible language for a company to build on.  For this reason, I'm choosing to inflate the value of foundry over Vyper above what the github metrics support, given that solidity has 97x the userbase than vyper according to repository count, making foundry a significantly more useful tool for our community.

According to clientdiversity.org, the go-ethereum client is used by around 43% of all validators, while besu is used by 16%. This suggests that geth is more relied upon ( more than 2x) and therefore deserves a higher credit. On the other hand, the percentage of validators using that client might be a very bad metric because not all clients are optimized for staking, e.g. focus more on being a reliable archive node. Furthermore, the goal of client diversity must be considered, thus, a client with 2x the market share among validators gets less than 2x the allocation.

While Solidity is of fundamental importance in ensuring a safe environment for app developers, lighthouse as the majority CL client, holding around 33% as of clientdiversity.org, deserves higher credit for it's fundamental importance for Ethereum's "life".

Hardhat as a tool used by various app developers who rely upon its safety deserves more credits than safe's safe-smart-account. However, safe is relied upon with huge amounts of money, which is harder to determine for hardhat, and thus is a fundamentally important piece of software many rely upon with their funds.

The open zeppelin contracts are highly valuable for app developers and significantly contribute to safety through setting very high standards.
Vyper still deserves more credits because its more general, being a programming language. As of today, programming on Ethereum is mainly done using Solidity, thus, vyper as an alternative should be funded to ensure its success.

Nethermind with 36% market share among Ethereum validators (base on clientdiversity.org), deserves more credits than the helios lightclient.
While lightclients are very important in the future, the nethermind client is already used and relied upon by over a third of the network, showcasing the trust users have in the software. On the other hand, if projects that are not-yet-live aren't funded in advance, they might never go live. Still, nethermind deserves more credits than helios.

These two feel harder to compare. I believe that nethermind wins simply because it represents 34% of the execution nodes running Ethereum and thus is a significant foundational pillar for the protocol.  Helios represents and ideological goal but I don't think it fully delivers it enough to be considered more important than Nethermind. I found it difficult to find any objective measure of how much helios is used for any production use cases. My intuition is that it is probably used very little, as the use of it represents an inherent *risk* or overhead, given that each call it makes requires the front-end to do extra work to verify the data, which for a front-end application means added latency and poor-er UX.  I love that Helios takes an ideological stand towards verified chain data, but I'm not convinced that it meaningfully solves the over-arching problem.  I chose to keep these two relatively close at a 2.5 multiplier as I think more projects like Helios need to be tried and experimented with.

Ape feels like a clear winner for the reason that it assists in smart contract development and is usable today. In contrast, Fe-lang has been "promised" for a long time but seems stuck in a cycle of perpetual development and unable to ship a usable version of their compiler.  Their github says that at present they are in the midst of a full re-write.  This suggests that Fe-lang continuing to exist only effects some possible future in which it turns out to be good and acquires users, where-as Ape is actively improving the ecosystem at the present time.

Comparing each project's space, contract verification is critical for users to confidently interact with Ethereum and avoid scams, while having robust and intelligently designed languages is useful to attract developers and minimize bugs. I'd give contract verification in general a 2x factor to start. Neither project is the leader in their particular space. Sourcify has some advantages over its competitors in that it is open-source, makes all data available publicly on IPFS, and supports full matches; this gives it a particularly useful niche in its space. Fe has seen limited use compared to other smart contract languages ( https://soliditylang.org/blog/2024/04/03/solidity-developer-survey-2023-results/ ), but does have a niche in being a Rust-like language, which may be useful in the future for attracting devs. But overall I'd say Sourcify is 5x more relevant in its space than Fe is within its.

Ethereum clients, both EL and CL, live in a space where diversity is extremely important, and I don't think adoption or market share is too useful as a metric, as long as the clients are both robust and viable for common use cases. The value difference in then in what they provide beyond just being stable options for clients. My impression is that the Prysm team have been leaders in broader Ethereum consensus design, on top of client development. Erigon is more known for their technical optimizations and focus on performance, where they have found success and pushed the broader EL space forward. I would value prysm's contributions higher, but not to an extreme extent.

Of course Solidity is more valuable to the success of Ethereum. It is the bedrock of Ethereum’s smart contract ecosystem, Alloy is a complementary tool that builds on Ethereum’s existing infrastructure, and mainly serve for Rust developers

Foundry serve for the smart contract reliability. which is a prerequisite for Ethereum’s credibility as a decentralized platform. By enabling faster iteration and rigorous testing, Foundry has directly reduced vulnerabilities in high-value DeFi and NFT projects.
    
Scaffold-ETH 2, while transformative for dApp prototyping, builds on existing tools like Foundry/Hardhat. Its value lies in accessibility, but it does not solve core challenges like contract security or gas optimization. Foundry’s influence permeates the entire ecosystem, including frameworks like Scaffold-ETH 2, making its contribution more systemic.
Remix’s accessibility and educational value make it a cornerstone of Ethereum’s developer ecosystem. Scaffold-ETH 2 enhances productivity but operates in a more specialized niche.

Solidity’s role in enabling Ethereum’s core functionality and securing its ecosystem makes it indispensable. Reth enhances network resilience but operates in a layer where alternatives exist. Thus, Solidity’s contribution is both broader and more foundational

While both projects are important, Reth’s role in client diversity, scaling breakthroughs, and infrastructure tooling directly impacts Ethereum’s survival and competitiveness, Reth’s optimizations push Ethereum’s throughput higher, which directly addressing execution-layer bottlenecks for L2 rollups. While Chains solves usability challenges but operates at a higher abstraction layer.

Reth’s modular design enables developers to reuse its components, including EVM, database bindings,  for diverse applications, whose contribution directly enhance Ethereum’s usability, decentralization, and scalability. Reth’s cross-cutting influence across Ethereum’s execution layer, developer ecosystem, and scalability roadmap. While Grandine strengthens consensus robustness.

Nimbus-eth2 directly underpins Ethereum’s consensus layer, a prerequisite for the network’s PoS functionality. Without robust clients like Nimbus, Ethereum’s scalability and security would falter. While Viem’s role in developer adoption and ecosystem growth is undeniable. Its TypeScript-first approach and performance optimizations reduce barriers to entry, fostering innovation in DeFi and NFTs.

Ethereum-lists/chains acts as the "plumbing" of the ecosystem, enabling interoperability at scale, while Viem is a specialized "tool" for developers. The former’s universal applicability and role in preventing fragmentation justify its higher multiplier.

While Lodestar strengthens Ethereum’s consensus layer, Reth’s role in decentralizing the execution layer and enabling next-generation infrastructure makes it disproportionately impactful and its broader applicability to Ethereum’s scalability roadmap.

Prysm operates at Ethereum’s protocol layer, ensuring network consensus and security. Nethereum, while valuable, operates at the application layer, enhancing developer experience but not directly sustaining the network

Web3.js has been foundational since Ethereum’s early days, enabling millions of developers to build DApps, wallets, and DeFi protocols. Its role in abstracting JSON-RPC complexities directly fueled Ethereum’s ecosystem growth, While Reth focuses on node optimization

Execution clients like Nethermind are foundational to Ethereum’s operation. Viem, while valuable, is a tool built atop this infrastructure. Nethermind’s impact on Ethereum’s security and decentralization is disproportionately larger. Viem streamlines development, but Nethermind ensures the network’s survival.

As one of Ethereum's primary consensus clients, Prysm has been critical to the Merge. While Ape enhances developer productivity, its role is supplementary. It streamlines workflows but does not affect Ethereum’s core consensus or network integrity

viem directly accelerates Ethereum development by simplifying complex interactions (e.g., contract calls, transaction signing) with intuitive, type-safe APIs. Sourcify remains indispensable for security but operates in a narrower scope.

Helios tackles a fundamental vulnerability in Ethereum’s infrastructure—centralized data providers. While Remix empowers developers, Helios ensures end-user security, a prerequisite for Ethereum’s adoption as a decentralized platform.

web3.py is indispensable for Python developers, enabling Ethereum’s integration into enterprise and open-source ecosystems. Fe’s impact is speculative, as most contracts still use Solidity/Vyper. Fe represents a visionary step toward secure smart contracts, web3.py’s immediate, tangible contributions to Ethereum’s developer ecosystem make it exponentially more impactful.

Prysm’s contribution is irreplaceable; Ethereum’s PoS consensus cannot function without clients like Prysm. Its codebase directly secures the network and processes billions in staked ETH 1210. Ethers.js, though widely adopted, is one of many tools that streamline development but do not define Ethereum’s core functionality.

ERC-4337 redefines user experience (UX) by eliminating reliance on externally owned accounts (EOAs), addressing critical issues like seed phrase vulnerabilities and gas management.While Erigon advances Ethereum’s infrastructure. The latter’s role in driving adoption and enhancing usability makes it the more pivotal contributor to Ethereum’s success.

Teku’s impact is structural, ensuring Ethereum’s core functionality and decentralization. Viem, while valuable, operates at a higher abstraction layer. Its contributions are complementary but not mission-critical; alternatives like ethers.js or web3.js can fulfill similar roles

Besu is core infrastructure ensuring Ethereum’s execution layer operates securely and efficiently. Although its node occupancy rate is only 16%, it has imprved EL client diversity. scaffold-eth-2 is just a toolkit for building decentralized applications (dapps).

Viem’s importance to Ethereum’s ecosystem surpasses Web3.js due to its protocol-aligned innovation, modern developer tooling, and active maintenance, contrasted with Web3.js’s stagnation and deprecation.

OpenZeppelin's importance to Ethereum’s ecosystem surpasses Remix due to its critical role, security leadership, and irreplaceable utility for smart contract developers, whereas Remix serves as a replaceable entry-level tool.
Lighthouse’s importance to Ethereum’s consensus layer eclipses besu's due to its broader adoption.
Consensys/Teku’s role in securing Ethereum’s consensus layer makes it 50x more vital than Safe-Smart-Account, which is a replaceable application-layer utility.

Alloy’s importance to Ethereum’s ecosystem doubles Reth’s due to its ubiquitous adoption and irreplaceable role in Rust-based tooling, whereas Reth remains a promising but unproven node client. It should be mentioned that Reth’s potential should not be understated.

OpenZeppelin Contracts’ infrastructure-critical role in securing and standardizing Ethereum’s smart contracts justifies its 2x greater importance. While Vyper enriches language diversity, its niche adoption and tooling gaps render it peripheral to Ethereum’s core mechanics.

OpenZeppelin Contracts’ irreplaceable role in securing Ethereum’s financial infrastructure justifies its 20x greater importance. While py-evm aids protocol research, its impact pales in comparison to OpenZeppelin’s ecosystem-wide, asset-critical contributions.

Titanoboa’s value lies in empowering Vyper developers, but its importance is derivative. Vyper’s direct impact on Ethereum’s security and financial infrastructure justifies its 10x greater significance.

Lodestar’s protocol-layer indispensability and role in Ethereum’s decentralization justify its 50x greater importance. While ethereum-lists/chains aids interoperability, its impact is peripheral compared to Lodestar’s foundational contributions to network security and resilience.

The importance of CL and EL is the same. However, Geth’s irreplaceable role in Ethereum’s execution layer and its ecosystem-wide dependencies justify its greater importance. While Nimbus enhances decentralization and accessibility, its impact is secondary to Geth’s foundational contributions.

Nethereum’s role in securing Ethereum’s .NET ecosystem and enabling enterprise adoption solidifies its 30x greater importance. While Ape enhances developer experience, its impact is peripheral and substitutable, unlike Nethereum’s foundational contributions to Ethereum’s infrastructure.

web3.py is 10x more critical than ethereum-lists/chains due to its irreplaceable role in enabling Python-based Ethereum integration. In contrast, ethereum-lists/chains  streamlines multi-chain configurations but does not directly enable core functionalities. Its maintenance is simpler.

OpenZeppelin Contracts’ irreplaceable role in securing Ethereum’s financial infrastructure solidifies its 20x greater importance. While Sourcify aids transparency, its impact is peripheral compared to OpenZeppelin’s foundational contributions to Ethereum’s security and standardization.
Hardhat’s role as one of the contract development backbones justifies its 10x greater importance over Scaffold-ETH 2, which has so many alternatives available.

Reth and Web3.py are equally important. Considering that Reth has many new features, although its market share is not large, its potential is enormous. And web3.py is crucial for Python ecosystem applications to integrate with Ethereum.

Prysm’s indispensable role in Ethereum’s consensus layer and its broad adoption solidify its primacy. While Reth showcases future potential, its experimental status and limited real-world impact render it secondary to Prysm’s foundational contributions.

Viem’s ecosystem-wide adoption and developer-centric innovation make it twice as vital as EthereumJS Monorepo, which remains confined to protocol research and tooling. The EthereumJS Monorepo is not used in mainstream Ethereum execution layer clients.

Ethers.js secures Ethereum’s dApp infrastructure, handling $500B+ in DeFi/NFT transactions daily. Remix, while user-friendly, is merely used to onboarding new smart contract developers and rarely used in production level smart contract development.

Grandine’s foundational role in Ethereum’s consensus layer and client diversity makes it irreplaceable. Titanoboa, while enhancing Vyper’s ecosystem, operates in a replaceable niche with indirect impact. Grandine’s protocol-critical contributions justify its 10x greater importance to Ethereum’s decentralized future.

Nimbus’s foundational role in Ethereum’s consensus layer and client diversity makes it irreplaceable. Titanoboa, while enhancing Vyper’s ecosystem, operates in a replaceable niche with indirect impact. Grandine’s protocol-critical contributions justify its 30x greater importance to Ethereum’s decentralized future. It is worth mentioning that Nimbus is more important than Grandine for its occupation and minimal resource comsumption.

Web3j’s foundational role in bridging Java applications to Ethereum’s decentralized future solidifies its 50x greater importance. Focuses on frontend interactions, a niche now dominated by ethers.js and Viem. Web3.js' absence would not disrupt and it has become obsolete due to maintenance neglect and competition.

Looking at github statistics for stars, dependent repositories, and dependent packages, suggests the multipliers of 2.66x, 1.77x, and 1.35x for safe being more valuable than alloy.  My intuition matches this, given that alloy is a lower level developer tool who's audience is constrained mostly to the rust ethereum ecosystem, where-as safe's audience is significantly broader.  The impact of truly secure smart contract wallets and account abstraction feels definitively larger to me.
Smart contracts data, specifically in a human-readable format is extremely important for retail adoption
Minority CL client. we need more of those
Core component to access web as a web2 developer
A minority CL client. We need more of those

Hardhat is very popular across the entire Ethereum ecosystem; AI estimates give it a market share of 30-70%. Ape has primarily been used by the Bored Ape Yacht Club and related projects, which are a relatively small share of the Ethereum ecosystem: their market cap is about ~150,000 ETH (giving it a share of 0.1% if you include ETH itself, and perhaps a few times more if you do not), and transaction volume is low. DappRadar ranks it as #542 in collectibles ( https://dappradar.com/dapp/bored-ape-yacht-club/nfts?range-ncc=all&range-ncmc=all&range-nc=all ). Adding on top other users beyond BAYC (eg. see a fork by Consensys: https://github.com/Consensys/apeworx-ape ), a ratio of 100 in Hardhat's favor seems reasonable.

Nethermind is pushing the limits to performance when it comes to client software. It can do more gas per second compared to both geth and reth. I consider performance important which is why I am ranking nethermind higher. Having some degree of competition between the client teams for performance is good for Ethereum. It is also the case that Nethermind supports rollup stacks, including Taiko and OP Stack. There are also more full nodes on the network that run Nethermind compared to Besu.

Comparing two categorically different repositories makes this much more of a value judgement. Ethereumjs has been useful for testing with Hardhat and also doing some more experimental client things. It has never been performant enough to actually run in production. Lighthouse is considered by many to be the standard consensus layer client. The correct functioning of lighthouse is extremely important to the Ethereum network functioning properly.

Categorically different. The openzeppelin contracts saved application layer developers a ton of time by not needing to always reinvent the wheel. They could focus on their app rather than implementing an ERC20 token correctly. This unlocked a ton of innovation at the app layer. I don't know how impactful the numbus team on the consensus layer.

Prysm was the leading consensus layer client for some time. The team still pushes forward very interesting improvements to the consensus layer, between prototyping FOCIL, ePBS and networking improvements. The account abstraction work is useful for onboarding and is becoming more impactful now that paymasters are being used more. I think that the account abstraction work will become more valuable over time given its so early in its adoption.

The Safe smart contract is one of the most impactful pieces of infrastructure in the entire Ethereum space. Safe just works and is battle tested to the point of securing billions of dollars for many different teams. It has become my go to way to interact with contracts, both on a professional and personal level. Alloy is an important library as the Rust ecosystem has helped to onboard many more core developers but its impact is still early compared to Safe.

Reth has kicked off a new wave of contributors to Ethereum core development. Many L2 teams want to adopt the Reth codebase. Rust has become the high status language to write Ethereum software in. Sourcify has yet to become standard. I don't even consider verifying my contracts on Sourcify after deploying them. I do agree that it is very important to have free software for verifying contracts but something is missing that is causing it to not be used.

Remix was important for early Ethereum development but it was never meant to be a super serious development environment. Safe is one of the most impactful projects out there due to securing billions of dollars across the ecosystem.

Without Solidity, the OZ contracts would not be possible. Solidity is one of the most impactful projects in the Ethereum space, due to its focus on correctness. Very few major bugs have happened with Solidity, keeping the ecosystem safe.

Nethermind is a production ready execution client while the ethereumjs execution client is more useful for testing and prototyping. Nethermind is significantly used to secure the live Ethereum network today

Gandine is much newer to the ecosystem while Lighthouse has been around for quite some time. Lighthouse is one of the more respected consensus layer clients and I know teams that prefer it over the others.
Prysm as a majority client is more valuable than Reth which isn't used in production by a significant number of validators/users.

Lighthouse as a majority consensus layer client deserves more credit than foundry, one of the most important libraries. This is because the success of Ethereum, safetywise, heavily depends on the reliability of majority clients.
ethers.js is used by more users and therefore deserves more credits.
Execution layer clients with a significant portion of the network relying on them deserve more credits than the python evm implementation.

Although client diversity is very important, as a certain number of clients is reached, every additional one might just slow down progress. While grandine is core-software, namely a CL client, it is not used by a significant number of users while web3.py sees wide-spread usage.

Go-ethereum as a majority EL client that Ethereum heavily relies on deserves more credits than a library used on the application layer because of the fundamental importance of go-ethereum for ethereum's safety.
The javascript library is more widespread and serves a larger user base.
Secure smart contracts are far harder to write and audit compared to a library that's mostly an interface.
The py-evm repository seems mostly stale and unused. So I don't see the value in supporting it as opposed to account abstraction work.
Lodestar is a full fledged client with testing, security, upgrade work and optimisations which are far harder to do compared to a JS library.
erigon is a client vs a JS library
nethermind is a client and that's far harder to do securely compared to web3 py
Foundry revolutionized the tooling landscape whereas vyper while novel only moderately improved the work solidity already had done

There's no question Hardhat has more contributors 382/142, more downloads 328k/?, more adoption, etc than Sourcify. It is intended for a broader audience. Ironically Hardhat has a Sourcify plugin so it's available to all Hardhat users. That said I'm giving weight to hardhat for the larger scope of the project and larger user footprint.

Regardless of adoption, both of these projects do all the same things and deserve as much funding for doing so. I'm rating prysm higher for its adoption but I don't think we need to give it more money.
It doesn’t seem ERC-4337 has been widely adopted, it is rather a research project which itself has value, but overall, the preference is to the Hardhat
Js ecosystem is larger in smart contracts. I've heard people using Viem, none using ape.

Ethers improved the DX from web3.js. It saved the Ethereum smart contract development at the right time.
Foundry is an improvement in the late game. I'd say the earlier project contributes more to the success of Ethereum

I like the early Remix project. It was the only possible way to see the inner working of smart contracts. The interface is a little bit frustrating to use but simple enough to reason.
I don't like the Remix today as it grew too complicated. It does have lots of ux improvement but very hard for beginers to get up to speed.

For Ethers.js, it's more like a good alternative of web3.js, but it's not the one and only project on the scene. In this sense, I'd say remix contributed more to the success of Ethereum.
Geth is the fully functional client in the early days. Ethereum wouldn't make it today without Geth

I select Lodestar over nethereum because client diversity is more valuable to me. CL client in Typescript can provide many utilities to web devs and in the future a safer light client on each user's browser. Nethereum is also great and is used by much more projects than Lodestar so I give a relatively low credit multiplier.
I select Grandine over Alloy because light client is more valuable to me. There aren't much CL light clients and one written in Rust would be very valuable to the ecosystem.

I select Erigon over Viem because client diversity is more valuable to me. Erigon has been able to prove itself worthy of a second Go client implementation and stand the test of time. On the other hand, Viem is very valuable to Typescript devs and is used by so many projects so I give a relatively low credit multiplier.

I select Nethermind over Remix because client diversity is more valuable to me. Nethermind has been steadily increasing it's popularity and market share while Remix is gradually been replaced by other dev tools like Hardhat or Foundry. Remix's advantage of being easy to onboard is not so appealing after other dev tools have made huge improvements over their DX. The credit multiplier does not get higher because Nethermind has reached 36% dominance and should not be difficult for it to get funding.

Github data suggests that these two are relatively equal, but my intuition says that Remix is more important by a decent margin.  The recent work on the EEELs python specification makes py-evm a less compelling project.  It is a dependency for eth-tester which itself is a dependency for at least one python-based smart contract development framework... but my understanding of remix is that it is the onramp for a lot of smart contract development and it's actual userbase is larger than the github stars or forks suggest.

Github data would suggest chains to be 16-26x more important, but I think this is massively skewed by this being a cross-chain project.  I would say that constraining to Ethereum as the scope and the nimbus-eth2 wins, though this is arbitrary and not anchored to any specific metric.  nimbus-eth2 seems to account for roughly 5% of the clients in the beacon network as the 3rd most used client.  Knowing the nimbus teams goal of shipping a unified client, I would say that the nimbus-eth2 client's importance is magnified simply in it being meaningfully *different* from the others in it's intended development path.

I would put Erigon as more important than Besu simply because it is architecturally different from the other EL clients.  My knowledge of Besu client architectrue is limited, but my understanding is that it is very similar to geth/nethermind and I'm not aware of any real advancements that the Besu team has provided in Ethereum research.  The Erigon team on the other hand is the most architecturally different EL client, which I think should be valued highly, as their architectural differences have yielded benefits such as the reverse-diff storage format for archival state data.

Using client diversity numbers, puts prysm at 25x times more important at 34.46% of the network as opposed to grandine's 1.3%.  Github stars give an 18x multiplier in favor of Prism.  More clients and more diversity would benefit our network, however, Teku, Prism, and Lighthouse all have reasonably close numbers so for the beacon network, this seems to not be a huge concern, thus I only adjusted the number downwards a little bit from the github and client diversity numbers.

Interestingly, github numbers do not agree with my assessment here.  Lighthouse currently holds 31.95% of the beacon network, where-as reth is 2% of the execution network.  However, github would suggest that reth should be 1.38x more important.  The reth project has created multiple high quality rust libraries that are important to the rust ecosystem and this should be taken into account.  It's unclear how to objectively settle on a number so this one is quite subjective.

The chains repository is a multi-chain project that spans beyond Ethereum.  I believe it is a useful and helpful tool for wallets that support Ethereum, but it seems less impactful to the Ethereum specific ecosystem.  The account-abstraction project is much more specific to Ethereum and has a significant userbase of over 10k repositories that seem to depend on it.  Account abstraction is important for Ethereum's UX, so a reliable and widely used library for this seems much more important than the chain list.
I am not sure if Fe is highly used today while ethereumjs has a clear application to me. I like the idea of EVM language diversity hence the multiplier isn’t high
I think Solidity is one of a few things made Ethereum successful. Majority of contracts that brought Ethereum’s adoption are written in this language
At this stage LH is one of the most popular beacon chain clients, one of the clients transitioned Ethereum to PoS
Teku is one of the clients that transition Ethereum to PoS, thus, it has had a higher impact on the success of Ethereum
Even though foundry is a great project, I am recalling times when geth was a single well supported Ethereum client which kept mainnet going

Github data seems to align pretty cleanly with my intuition.  Stars and fork numbers suggest between 4.0x-4.83x multiplier.  I chose to round up because I'm not convinced that we need another CL client, though if their performance numbers prove viable in a more widespread deployed production environment, I'd probably re-assess this.

This one is hard to compare.  They exist across very different axis of usage.  Github numbers suggest web3.py should be as much as 27x more valuable, and my intuition and attempt to reason about this suggest that this isn't entirely wrong.  Python is a very widely use scripting language and web3.py is the goto library for this.  However, given that web3.py has been around for many years, and grandine is new, I think the github data is heavily skewed towards web3.py.

I believe Alloy is four times more valuable to Ethereum’s success than Erigon.

Erigon is just one of the execution clients and holds about a 3% market share. In contrast, Alloy is a Rust library for interacting with Ethereum. Since they’re quite different, let’s compare them from a few angles.

First, consider GitHub stars. As of February 2025, Erigon has 3.3k stars while Alloy has 0.8k stars, meaning Erigon’s count is roughly 4.13 times higher. However, it’s important to remember that GitHub stars accumulate over time, and Erigon is an older project.

Next, on a more subjective level: Erigon, despite its low market share, plays an important role in ensuring diversity among clients. On the other hand, Alloy—being a complete rewrite of ethers‑rs—is becoming the de facto standard for Ethereum interaction libraries in Rust.

I personally believe Rust will be an increasingly important language in the blockchain world due to its excellent performance and growing adoption across many areas. As the importance of Rust engineers rises, so too will the significance of Alloy.

While Erigon may have more GitHub stars, when considering these broader factors, I think Alloy offers four times the value to Ethereum’s success compared to Erigon.

I believe that Helios is eight times more valuable to Ethereum's success than Sourcify.

Helios is a light client implementation written in Rust, while Sourcify is a tool for verifying smart contract code. Since these two serve different purposes, they can’t be directly compared, so I’ll compare them from a few perspectives.

First, consider GitHub stars. As of February 2025, Helios has 1.9k stars compared to Sourcify’s 0.8k, making Helios 2.4 times more popular. Additionally, the GitHub API shows that Sourcify was created in April 2019, whereas Helios was launched in August 2022. This means that despite being newer, Helios has managed to gather more stars.

Next, from a more subjective viewpoint: Helios, developed by a16z, supports both Ethereum and OP Stack chains. Increasing the number of users running light clients is crucial for Ethereum’s future because it reduces the need to trust RPC providers and helps promote decentralization. On the other hand, Sourcify’s role in verifying smart contract code is useful in addressing the centralization seen in services like Etherscan, but its overall importance might not be as significant.

For these reasons, I believe that Helios is eight times more valuable than Sourcify.

I believe Foundry is 21 times more valuable than Teku for Ethereum’s success.

Teku is a consensus client built in Java, while Foundry is a tool that helps with developing smart contracts. They aren’t directly comparable, so I’ll consider both objective and subjective factors.

First, objectively, let’s look at GitHub stars. As of February 2025, Teku has about 700 stars, while Foundry has around 8,600 stars—that’s roughly 12.3 times more. Moreover, according to the GitHub API, Teku was created in September 2018, but Foundry was launched in September 2021, which makes Foundry’s star count even more impressive given its shorter lifespan.

Subjectively, Teku is just one of several consensus clients and is the third most used. Foundry, on the other hand, is a highly useful tool for writing smart contracts in Solidity. Although Hardhat used to be the de facto standard, Foundry has essentially replaced it. It even allows you to write tests in Solidity and, being built in Rust, it’s very fast. I believe Foundry will play a major role in the future of the Ethereum ecosystem—much more so than Teku.

For these reasons, I conclude that Foundry is 21 times more valuable than Teku.

I believe that Solidity is 13 times more valuable to Ethereum’s success than Fe.

Solidity is one of the smart contract languages built in C++, while Fe is also a smart contract language but is built in Rust. I’ll explain my reasoning with both objective and subjective points.

Objective Comparison:
Looking at GitHub stars as of February 2025, Solidity has 23.9k stars compared to Fe’s 8.6k. In addition, when checking repository creation dates via the GitHub API, Teku (associated with Solidity) was created in September 2018, whereas Foundry (associated with Fe) was created in September 2021. This shows that Foundry is newer yet has managed to gather a significant number of stars.

Subjective Assessment:
Solidity is, without a doubt, the de facto standard for implementing EVM contracts. There is a robust ecosystem built around Solidity, and with trends like zkVM emerging, the specific language used may eventually become less critical. Although Fe might have some superior features compared to Solidity, I personally don’t see it having as much impact on Ethereum’s future success.

For these reasons, I conclude that Solidity is 13 times more valuable to Ethereum’s success than Fe.

Teku is a pretty important consensus layer that I know lots of people run, while at the same time, FE is a language with no real usage. Personally, I've never seen or audited any real contracts with FE; their Devcon booth was always empty, proving no real "devs" were on board. There's still a long way to go to get FE to the stage where it realistically has an impact in production.

py-EVM helps many Python development tools to be built, while the AA repository is basically an "ERC" implementation. I don't think AA has proven its success yet; the ecosystem is still heavily centralized. The good news is that we do have a roadmap to open that up. That being said, I haven't seen anything uniquely created by the 4337 ERC that relates to Ethereum's success, while py-EVM is a solid dependency

They're equally irrelevant in 2025; Hardhat is a dead framework now. Consider the significant performance issues compared to Foundry. Scaffold-eth-2 is only used for hackathon projects nowadays and for educational purposes. However, since mid-2024, every frontend can easily be spun up with AI. I think Scaffold is still good for learning about subgraphs, contracts, and their relationships as a kickstarter, but its importance has definitely dropped.

Scaffold-eth-2 must have onboarded so many more developers than Remix. Remix was good, but it's not relevant anymore since around 2022. If we consider the number of developers joining before and after 2022, it's reasonable to assume that many more people learned from the Scaffold-eth-2 framework. Another very important aspect of Scaffold is that it's not just about contracts; it includes a front end, subgraph, and contract starter kit. This teaches people the relationships between the components involved in developing real-world dapps. For that reason, I think it's much more realistic and has helped many more people gain knowledge and become genuinely helpful—not just playing around with Remix and deploying ERC-20 tokens.

I generally find execution clients to be more valuable because of the broader ecosystem that uses them. However, for this pair, considering the market share, Prysm is more significant in the market share (30%+ of consensus clients) compared to Reth's 2%.

I'd argue that ethers.js was one of the most important packages because literally every single web3 frontend relies on it. Its importance has dropped due to the rise of Viem and other tools, but I think every DeFi app was built on top of ethers.js in 2020 (when the wagmi framework used it), and even today at least 50% of them are built with ethers.js. 

Lighthouse is a very important client, but its impact is not as direct as a package that is used by every single JS Dapps

I think the 4337 (AA) repo is a very big and ambitious attempt to "solve the wallet problem" openly and collaboratively. I don't think we've seen proof that this has "succeeded" yet; instead, the AA landscape is still extremely centralized at this moment: people are not agreeing on standards, and the biggest UX improvements have not come from this ERC protocol but instead from individual wallets. I do believe they have the potential to play a much bigger role here, and it's important to keep it going because it voices the "value" of Ethereum, which is pushing something out collaboratively. I think the signal itself is valuable to the DApp/DeFi community.

OpenZeppelin is a very important (if not the most important) repository that has contributed enormously to the success of contract development on the EVM. It's not just about templates and safety; they provide many utilities that improve UX, teaching developers how to easily extend their contracts with features that make them not just more secure but more usable. For example, they offer proxy patterns, access control contracts, storage utilities, and ERC712 signing functionality. Without OpenZeppelin, we wouldn't have so many DApps agreeing on ERC712 signatures, and we would experience far more hacks with upgradeable contracts and storage updates. Even with high-quality developers, OpenZeppelin's contribution is massive. I don't believe Erigon has an impact close to the level of OpenZeppelin, and I think it's hard to find any others with the same level of significant impact, too.

Viem is now the number one JS library for Ethereum. Not only must TypeScript/JS applications use it, but all the SDKs and frameworks built on top of it as well. For example, Wagmi, the largest React library, and RainbowKit wallet SDKs: every frontend now uses these frameworks to connect different wallets and handle on-chain hooks. The JS community is by far the largest in terms of DApp development for Ethereum; web apps all rely on it, and there are many TypeScript packages available for liquidation bots, trading bots, and CLI tools, all mostly in JS (TS), with Viem being the package behind all of them.
Vyper is a programming language that is known to run in production
Fe is not in production
I wish that sourcify took off but its not used that much, the canonical list of chains is used
Besu is important for securing the network. They have about the same number of stars and forks on Github
I know of people using titanoboa but do not know anybody using grandine
Safe is one of the most important apps in all of ethereum

Between OpenZeppelin and Grandine, I believe OpenZeppelin plays a more important role in supporting, testing, and developing application standards that drive Ethereum adoption. While Grandine is an optimized and parallelized consensus client, contributing to client diversity, there are multiple alternatives available for consensus layer (CL) clients.

Both Ethers.js and Helios contribute significantly to the Ethereum ecosystem, but Helios deserves more funding because it is a trustless, efficient, and portable multichain light client written in one of the most robust language -  Rust.
With it's ability to syncs in seconds, requiring no storage, and lightweight enough to run on mobile devices will help more users to join with minimal hardware requirement. It may have more implementation including direct embed in wallets and dapps.

While Ethers.js is important tool for Ethereum developers, Helios fills the gap in Ethereum’s decentralization and security. Funding Helios would directly support Ethereum’s long-term vision.

I believe ethereum-lists/chains deserves more recognition than Erigon because, while Erigon contributes to client diversity, ethereum-lists/chains provides unique and valuable information that developers can use for building projects and applications.

I feel Helios deserves more credit than Solidity because of its practical applications and unique impact on the ecosystem.

While Solidity is essential as the primary smart contract language for Ethereum, it is already well-established and widely recognized. On the other hand, Helios introduces a trustless, efficient, and portable multichain light client, which plays a crucial role in improving Ethereum accessibility and decentralization.

Go-Ethereum (Geth) is the mainstream execution client officially maintained by Ethereum, developed in the Go programming language. It handles transactions, executes smart contracts, and synchronizes blockchain data, making it one of the most widely-used node software on the Ethereum mainnet. Developers and users can utilize Geth to set up nodes, deploy private chains, or deeply interact with the Ethereum network. Currently, Geth dominates the market with a 43% share, holding a significant advantage. Therefore, maintaining the stability and security of Geth is crucial for Ethereum.

Reth, implemented in Rust for the Ethereum protocol, provides numerous modular features that allow developers to leverage it to build node-related applications such as indexers, MEV-searchers, and more. Although reth's adoption rate is not yet high, as fundamental infrastructure, its future impact remains profound. Sourcify provides contract verification which is quite important, but it functions more as a tool. While it significantly helps the ecosystem, its scope for expansion is limited.

It's a bit difficult to choose. Viem is more like an application-layer development tool, while Prysm is a low-level consensus tool. Viem is more widely used, but Prysm requires research and development to maintain and develop in order to maintain the entire Ethereum protocol's consensus. Maintaining consensus is fundamental to Ethereum. Based on the fact that Viem could easily be replaced by other tools in the future (for example, how Viem has replaced ethers.js), I choose Prysm.

Viem provides developer-friendly tools that enable rapid development of DApps or applications; Sourcify ensures contract verification security. In comparison, Viem has a greater impact. If Viem becomes compromised, it would affect more projects, whereas contract verification can be supported by other tools and is not necessarily dependent on Sourcify.

Overall, the vision and impact of Account Abstraction (AA) are no less significant than Execution Layer (EL) clients. In order to achieve mass adoption of Ethereum, the establishment of AA standards and direction has an even more far-reaching influence. It enables users to have better control of their assets, reducing dependence on exchanges. At the same time, it provides a better user experience, with account management and recovery features, and even has long-term implications for Decentralized Identity (DID) and privacy.

From a diversity perspective, while Execution Layer (EL) already has multiple different implementations, there is currently no mainstream reference for Account Abstraction (AA) contract wallet implementations, especially for the ERC-4337 contract implementation. At the system and contract code levels, there are too many constraints and unfriendly aspects. Safe-smart-account, with its accumulated engineering experience, has proposed a contract implementation that does not fully comply with ERC-4337, which serves as a good reference indicator for the current immaturity of AA contract wallets. Even if it does not become a reference standard in the future, the Safe contract is currently an important multi-signature wallet application for contract accounts, with many assets being protected through it.

geth has been refined over many years, having gone through multiple fork upgrades. In terms of code quality and depth of consensus layer implementation, it has become a crucial learning and reference benchmark for developers. Simultaneously, many of its modules have been extracted to develop more node-related applications.

A difficult choice. Based on the future impact of AA contract accounts, I chose safe-smart-account. However, ethereumjs still has significant influence in terms of JavaScript implementation of the EVM. Yet, because there are implementations in different languages, diversity is ensured. Safe-smart-account is a project that can push Ethereum to its next milestone.

Vyper is another important contract development language choice besides Solidity, and it became an alternative for security during the peak of DeFi applications. Currently, Solidity remains the mainstream contract language, with Vyper following. However, from a security perspective, Vyper has its unique characteristics. Although ethers.js is more popular among developers, from a foundational perspective, Vyper has its necessary place of existence.
The standards and vision of Account Abstraction (AA) far exceed protocol libraries, and such tools have implementations in various programming languages.

As both are clients, Besu is a long-developed project and is particularly beloved by permissioned blockchain enthusiasts. Especially in enterprise and banking sectors, those who prefer developing in Java are fond of the Besu client. Over the past few years, Besu has also adopted various permissioned blockchain consensus algorithms. Based on these factors and considering the adoption rate, I choose the Besu project.

A difficult choice, because both projects are valuable. Lighthouse is a popular CL client, but web3.py has existed for a long time and was an important early reference implementation of the web3 package. It has influenced many projects, with many projects being built based on the web3.py package. Particularly, web3.py's code and architecture are not only concise but also have very comprehensive testing. Considering that Lighthouse has diversity with multiple clients available, that's why I chose web3.py.

py-evm is undoubtedly an important reference implementation of the Ethereum protocol, but considering the usage rate of Solidity, with large-scale applications built upon it, and its influence on later contract languages like MOVE and Cairo, coupled with having 10 times more stars on GitHub compared to py-evm, I chose it based on its scale and impact.

Account Abstraction (AA) contract wallets represent a paradigm shift in account models and are fundamental infrastructure that can enable Ethereum to achieve mass adoption. From the perspective of impact and standard-setting, while Remix indeed provided excellent assistance to newcomers in early contract application development with its visualization tools and user-friendly contract interaction interface, the emergence of various packages and tools like foundry/hardhat has provided alternative solutions. Therefore, considering the overall importance, I choose AA.

Web3j serves as the Ethereum client's Java implementation package and is widely used, particularly in Android applications and consortium blockchain adoption. Almost all Java developers encounter it, especially in enterprise and banking institutions. While ChainID Network is an excellent metadata collection page that allows users to easily obtain network information, it is essentially just a data format that can be easily replaced. Therefore, I choose web3j.

Besu is an Ethereum client popular among Java developers with many advocates using it. However, Helios, as a light client, can provide users with more decentralized and self-verifiable transaction capabilities in the future. Additionally, it can support L2-interop, which will further promote a unified Ethereum. Therefore, I choose Helios based on its potential impact and importance.

Web3j is a Java implementation of the web3 package that provides a client for interacting with the Ethereum protocol. Scaffold-eth-2, on the other hand, offers a foundational framework that allows dapp developers to quickly have a complete functional environment, especially common in hackathon competitions, serving as a Swiss Army knife for the frontend. I believe scaffold-eth-2, as an all-in-one development environment, provides an excellent foundation for dapp developers just entering the development space. It includes many web3 concepts such as ENS, wallet interactions, and contract interactions. Compared to web3 packages, it has a greater impact in helping more people understand the Ethereum development ecosystem.

Alloy not only provides web3 toolkits but also offers infrastructure-related tool interfaces, such as useful tools for KMS, MEV, and txpool. However, considering OpenZeppelin, which is a contract library that contract developers in the industry must reference and cite, it provides simple and battle-tested contract code. Additionally, it has been challenged by the community, dapps, and security audit teams, helping more dapp developers avoid writing unqualified and fragile code. As an open-source project with high impact in the industry, compared to toolkits with alternative projects, OpenZeppelin has protected many assets from theft. Therefore, considering the number of GitHub stars and forks, I give OpenZeppelin a weight of 30.

py-evm, as an early Python reference implementation of the Ethereum protocol, provides considerable value. It is readable and easy for other language implementations to reference, and the project management and testing are quite comprehensive. Erigon, as an EL client implementation, while not as ubiquitous as Nethermind or Geth, is still a continuously operating full node service. Considering that py-evm has not been as actively developed in recent years, while Erigon continues to evolve and develop, I therefore choose Erigon.

AA contract wallets represent a paradigm shift in account models and are fundamental infrastructure that can enable Ethereum to achieve mass adoption. From the perspective of impact and standard-setting, Vyper as a contract language has already been overshadowed by Solidity's widespread adoption, and its substituability is not as strong. Overall, in terms of impact, standard-setting, and prevalence, AA contract wallets are more important and far-reaching.

Foundry, as a contract development framework, provides many convenient tools - from testing tools, high-performance environments, friendly debuggers, contract calldata encoding tools, and providing fork environments, which greatly improved the inefficient environments developers previously experienced with Truffle/Hardhat. Viem, as a TypeScript interface, provides many application developers with simple syntax to quickly integrate contracts and protocols. However, in terms of overall tool completeness, Foundry continues to iterate and develop, integrating more tools to help contract developers save more time. Considering convenience and impact, I choose Foundry.

Lighthouse, as a consensus client, is a core infrastructure of Ethereum and holds a significant market share.Although Ethers.js is widely used, there are still many alternative solutions with similar functionalities.

Prysm is a full Ethereum consensus client focused on implementing the Beacon Chain protocol, widely deployed on the mainnet and directly involved in network consensus.The ethereumjs-monorepo is more of a modular implementation of protocol-level components, suited for research, testing, or lightweight client development, rather than mainnet-level consensus execution.In terms of actual consensus protocol operations, Prysm plays a more critical role
Java- and Android-based development is more widely used and feature-rich at the application layer compared to Python-based applications.
Geth holds a significant share of the market
The closer a component is to developers and end users, the greater its weight.

Ethers.js is a general-purpose, infrastructure-level development tool with extremely wide adoption, while eth-infinitism is more of a specialized implementation within the ERC-4337 ecosystem — it offers deep functionality but is not as broadly used. In terms of adoption breadth, Ethers.js carries more weight.

account-abstraction is a core project in the Ethereum ecosystem driving the implementation of Account Abstraction (ERC-4337), aiming to improve the Ethereum account model by making smart contract accounts as flexible and user-friendly as externally owned accounts. foundry-rs/foundry is a Rust-based Ethereum smart contract development toolkit designed for high performance, modularity, and an optimized developer experience. In terms of Ethereum’s core infrastructure, Account Abstraction is more important.

remix-project is an officially supported web-based integrated development environment (IDE) for Ethereum, designed specifically for writing, compiling, deploying, and debugging smart contracts. The closer a tool is to developers and users, the greater its weight.
holds a significant share of the market

Vyper is a smart contract programming language for Ethereum, with Python-like syntax that emphasizes simplicity, security, and auditability. Remix Project is an officially supported web-based integrated development environment (IDE) for Ethereum, designed specifically for writing, compiling, deploying, and debugging smart contracts. The IDE is more important than the language itself, as there are many alternative languages available.
Ethereum's core development language is very important

Titanoboa is a Python toolkit for testing and simulating Vyper smart contracts. Grandine is a lightweight Ethereum client that directly participates in core functions of the Ethereum network such as consensus, block synchronization, and transaction processing. From the perspective of core functionality and operation of an Ethereum client, Grandine is more critical.
In terms of market share, Erigon has more credits
Nethereum is a tool geared toward application development, while py-evm is a low-level implementation focused on protocol research; for developers, Nethereum has more credits.
Account Abstraction is one of the key features in Ethereum's core protocol upgrades.
Nimbus-eth2 is a lightweight Ethereum consensus client that supports the Ethereum PoS network and is optimized for low-resource devices. It is the only consensus client that supports such devices
more widely used
Core client with a high market share.
Core client with a high market share.

Vyper is the second most used language to develop smart contracts. It is used by large and battle tested projects like Curve. On the other hand, Remix serves rather small amount of devs comparing to Foundry and Hardhat. Even though it has a UI and plugins, they are still not intuitive to use.

web3.py is used by many smart contract devs and many great libraries built on top of it. Nimbus contributes to the multi-clients aspect of Ethereum security but if we are talking about contribution to the success of Ethereum, then unfortunately Nimbus is at a disadvantage comparing to web3.py

ape is great library for DApp devs while Nethermind clients contribute a lot to multi-clients security of Ethereum. But in terms of contribution to the success of Ethereum, I will pick ape over Nethermind.
Sourcify is like the multi-client version of smart contracts explorer but sourcify is rather new and small compared to Etherscan.
Foundry is just great tools for DApp devs and it definitely helps a lot of projects shipped their products. Nimbus contributes to the security of Ethereum.

account-abstraction marks a significant step towards the end game of Ethereum's wallet and user experience while ape is a great tool for python devs. account-abstraction shapes the foundation for wallets which will impact millions of users.

Hardhat has helped hundreds of projects ship their products. Lighthouse on the other hand contributes the security of Ethereum a lot as it is second most popular CL client. Unfortunately if we are talking about contribution to the success of Ethereum, I would pick Hardhat over Lighthouse.
account-abstraction lays the foundation for wallets which in turn impacts millions of users while scaffold-eth-2 helps new devs to onboard more quickly.

Safe has contributed a lot to the development of smart contract wallet and it helps secure billions of dollars of assets. Without it, we will probably hear more hacks due to private key or mnemonic words theft. Sourcify contributes to the security of smart contract explorer but it's rather new and small compared to Etherscan.
Prysm is the most popular CL client while Nethermind is the second most popular EL client. However since EL impacts a lot more users than CL, I would reduce the multiplier from 3 to 1.2.
account-abstraction lays the foundation for wallets which in turn impacts millions of users while vyper helps many projects build their smart contracts with Python.

chains list is usefule for some explorers, wallets or DApps but it is not a necessity to the ecosystem while Erigon serves as a EL client, contributing to the security of Ethereum and has ported many improvements back to go-ethereum
Solidity is too crucial to Ethereum. Without it, there won't be any projects built on Ethereum. However Prysm is the largest CL client so without it CL would be more vulnerable.

Foundry has helped hundreds of projects shipped their products while Lodestar contributes to the security of Ethereum. However, if we are talking about contribution to the success of Ethereum, I will give Foundry much more credits.

openzeppelin contracts have saves smart contract devs so much time on writing libraries, especially safe ones. Without them, there will not be so many projects and many of them will get hacked. On the other hand, reth is quickly rising as a performant new client and the team has done some important research on the limits of current Ethereum/EVM. reth is also being used by many teams building many different tools, e.g., MEV tools, simulation tools ,indexers etc.

I will choose openzeppelin contracts over reth because reth is rather new but due to it's potential impact on the infrastructure of the ecosystem, I pick a small multiplier.

go-ethereum is the most popular EL client while foundry is the most popular smart contract dev tool. Without go-ethereum, Ethereum will suffer from more attacks and malicious hardforks, that's why I pick go-ethereum over foundry. There is also good replacement for foundry like Hardhat.
This number reflects Lighthouse’s irreplaceable role in running the Ethereum network vs. Alloy’s strong, but replaceable, role in development tooling.

web3.py is ~14× more useful because it is the indispensable, default bridge between Python (a major language in data science, finance, and backend systems) and Ethereum, whereas viem, while modern and well-designed, is a replaceable convenience layer in a TypeScript ecosystem already well-served by mature alternatives like ethers.js.

Reflects Besu’s critical role as a versatile, enterprise-grade Ethereum client supporting both public and private networks, compared to Remix’s function as a development tool that, while valuable, has alternative solutions available. This allows Eth to scale into the real world / enterprise

Nethermind directly supports the core operation and decentralization of Ethereum as a widely-used execution client, whereas Alloy provides helpful—but replaceable—tooling in a single-language ecosystem.

While Remix is a powerful educational and prototyping tool, Nethermind directly powers the Ethereum network itself as an execution client, making it foundational and irreplaceable at a protocol level.

reth directly supports the Ethereum network’s infrastructure by providing a high-performance, modular client, enhancing network resilience and decentralization. In contrast, vyper offers a valuable but alternative approach to smart contract development, with a more limited adoption compared to the dominant Solidity language.

go-ethereum is a cornerstone of the Ethereum network, directly facilitating its core operations and maintaining network integrity. In contrast, ape serves as a valuable development tool that enhances the smart contract development process but does not play a critical role in the network’s foundational infrastructure.

While both clients contribute to Ethereum’s network diversity and resilience, lighthouse’s emphasis on speed and security, along with its broader adoption, grants it a higher relative usefulness compared to lodestar.

Chainlist allows users to automatically add networks to their wallet, reducing the need to copy-paste chain IDs and RPC URLs manually. This is more efficient and safer for users. It directly facilitates user adoption, which is a critical blocker for crypto

Without ethers.js much of today's cryptocurrency dapps would not exist. While UX for users is important (Chainlist), without apps there would be no users. Users of Chainlist could use other sources to get the data they would get from Chainlist
Remix is a foundational tool.

While Fe introduces a promising new language for smart contract development, its current adoption and impact are limited. In contrast, Titanoboa provides immediate, practical benefits by enhancing the development experience for existing Vyper users, thereby contributing more significantly to the current Ethereum development ecosystem.
Hardhat came first, even if foundry is much better.
Safe smart wallets are a critical component for a wide range of users, including individuals, DAOs, and enterprises. Scaffold has many alternatives.
Safe smart wallets are a critical component for a wide range of users, including individuals, DAOs, and enterprises. Scaffold has many alternatives.

Teku serves as a critical component in Ethereum’s consensus mechanism, directly impacting network security and decentralization, especially for institutional participants. In contrast, Helios provides valuable, trustless access to Ethereum for users seeking lightweight solutions, but its role is more supplementary compared to the foundational importance of a consensus client like Teku.

OpenZeppelin Contracts serves as a critical foundation for secure and standardized smart contract development across the Ethereum ecosystem, with widespread adoption and dependency. In contrast, while Alloy provides valuable tools for Rust developers interacting with Ethereum, its impact and adoption are more specialized and limited in scope.

Scaffold-ETH 2 provides a comprehensive and integrated development environment that accelerates the creation and deployment of decentralized applications, directly impacting the growth and innovation within the Ethereum ecosystem. In contrast, Py-EVM serves a more specialized role, offering a Python-based implementation of the Ethereum Virtual Machine primarily for research and protocol development purposes.

While Ape enhances the development experience for creating and interacting with smart contracts, the ethereum-lists/chains repository is an authoritative source of chain information, replacing this repository would require significant effort in data collection and maintenance.

Prysm serves as a critical component in Ethereum’s consensus mechanism, directly impacting network security and decentralization. In contrast, Titanoboa provides valuable tools for Vyper developers, enhancing smart contract development and testing, but its impact is more specialized within the developer community.

This is quite a disparate comparison. Safe is prominent as the leading multisig platform, and its success so far provides value for its users as well as the broader reputation of Ethereum as a whole as a secure place to store high-value assets. As for Helios, I am having difficulty finding statistics around real-world usage, even for the broader light client space as a whole. Ethereum today is not particularly light-client-friendly, and it may be difficult for modern light clients to provide much value, through no fault of their own. Nevertheless, Safe seems to have provided much more tangible benefit to Ethereum as a whole as of now, though that number is difficult to quantify.

web3.js has been integral since Ethereum’s inception (2015), enabling developers to build DApps, interact with smart contracts, and manage wallets. Its APIs became the de facto standard for Ethereum JavaScript development, powering critical infrastructure like MetaMask and Truffle, while Helios addresses a specific trust vector.

Lodestar is a core component of Ethereum’s consensus layer, which is indispensable for network security and scalability. While Safe is a smart contract-based wallet framework designed for AA, enabling features like multi-signature security, customizable transaction guards, and modular extensions. Lodestar’s contribution to consensus mechanism is foundational and non-negotiable. Safe Smart Account, while transformative for user adoption, builds atop this infrastructure.

Reth contributes to Ethereum’s infrastructure scalability and L2 adoption. Its performance optimizations directly address Ethereum’s gas throughput limitations, enabling cheaper and faster transactions. Vyper strengthens Ethereum’s security foundation by reducing smart contract exploits, fostering trust in decentralized applications.

Execution clients like Nethermind are essential for Ethereum’s day-to-day operations. Nethermind’s adoption reduces reliance on Geth, mitigating single-point-of-failure risks. While EthereumJS enables developer innovation.

Erigon revolutionized execution-layer scalability by drastically reducing node sync times and storage demands. By making full nodes accessible to more operators, it strengthened Ethereum’s execution-layer security and reliability, Nimbus directly enabled Ethereum’s transition to PoS by providing a resource-efficient consensus client, Both projects are essential, but Erigon’s execution-layer optimizations have a broader, more foundational impact on Ethereum’s functionality and adoption.

Reth is a high-performance Ethereum execution client written in Rust, designed to improve network infrastructure. While Scaffold-ETH-2 is a developer toolkit focused on simplifying dApp development on Ethereum. Reth’s role in securing Ethereum’s base layer provides disproportionately higher value.

Fe is a statically typed, Rust-inspired smart contract language designed for Ethereum.  Web3j is a Java/Android library for interacting with Ethereum nodes and smart contracts. Web3j has immediate impact on Ethereum’s developer, which has enabled thousands of projects since 2016, whereas Fe’s contributions are still emerging

Ethers.js serves as an essential foundation, while Hardhat acts as a productivity booster. Though Hardhat speeds up development, Ethers.js fundamentally enables the process. For each project utilizing Hardhat, numerous others depend purely on Ethers.js for vital functions.

While web3.py democratizes Ethereum development, Teku plays a crucial role in securing and scaling the network following the Merge, making it essential. Teku's focus on enterprise solutions has institutionalized Ethereum staking, which attracts institutional capital and stabilizes the network's economic model. In contrast, web3.py has a more limited impact, primarily serving the needs of Python developers rather than the broader Ethereum network.

Chains plays a crucial role in promoting ecosystem growth, while Erigon is essential for enhancing Ethereum’s functionality by facilitating efficient node operation and scalability. Erigon's technical improvements specifically tackle key challenges faced by Ethereum, including node accessibility and state bloat. In contrast, while Ethereum-Lists/Chains addresses coordination issues, it does not enhance the resilience of the protocol at the fundamental level.
Ethers.js directly enables millions of dApps, wallets, and tools, forming the backbone of Ethereum’s JavaScript ecosystem. Fe’s impact is theoretical and unproven.

Nimbus-eth2 is indispensable to Ethereum’s decentralization and accessibility, while Grandine remains a supplementary option for high-performance scenarios. Nimbus-eth2’s lightweight architecture (1 GiB memory) enables low-cost hardware like Raspberry Pi to run nodes, democratizing participation and strengthening censorship resistance—a core Ethereum value.

Lighthouse’s role in Ethereum’s operational survival, decentralization, and security dwarfs EthereumJS’s utility. The 10x importance ratio reflects Lighthouse’s irreplaceability as infrastructure versus EthereumJS’s supplementary developer tools. While both projects contribute to the ecosystem, Lighthouse’s impact on Ethereum’s core functionality is exponentially greater.

Ethers.js is Ethereum’s present lifeline, while ERC-4337 is a future enabler. The 100x importance ratio reflects Ethers.js’s irreplaceable role in daily operations versus ERC-4337’s nascent, unproven utility. For Ethereum’s survival today, Ethers.js is indispensable; for its evolution tomorrow, ERC-4337 is optional.

Alloy secures Ethereum’s developer ecosystem. Without it, dApps, wallets, and infrastructure (e.g., Foundry) would collapse. ERC-4337, while transformative, remains optional for Ethereum’s current operations.
Remix simplifies development but is non-essential. Developers can use CLI tools, Foundry, or other IDEs without impacting Ethereum’s functionality.

vyperlang/vyper’s value far exceeds its current adoption rate. As Solidity’s only substantive alternative, Vyper ensures Ethereum has contingency plans against language-level vulnerabilities. Its security-centric design offers superior options for high-risk contracts. While wevm/viem enhances development efficiency, its tooling role can be substituted by other libraries, whereas Vyper’s role in ecosystem defense is irreplaceable.
Lighthouse’s importance to Ethereum’s consensus layer eclipses Teku’s due to its broader adoption.
Prysm’s importance to Ethereum dwarfs Web3.js by orders of magnitude due to its protocol-critical role, irreplaceable function, and ongoing maintenance, whereas Web3.js has become obsolete.

Geth is core infrastructure ensuring Ethereum’s execution layer operates securely and efficiently. Geth’s dominance in node distribution (~43% of execution clients) makes its stability paramount. HardHat is a contract development tool while  Foundry’s rise has reduced its uniqueness.

Nethermind is core infrastructure ensuring Ethereum’s execution layer operates securely and efficiently. Nethermind's dominance in node distribution (~36% of execution clients) makes its stability paramount. HardHat is a contract development tool while Foundry’s rise has reduced its uniqueness.
Makes smart contracts data accessible for anyone

Erigon and Besu are both execution clients. Today, Erigon is 3% of the network and Besu is 16%. We can also analyze their contributions to client diversity by looking at the Herfindahl index of execution clients with and without each one:

>>> def hh(l): return sum([x**2 for x in l]) / sum(l)**2
... 
>>> hh([43,36,16,3,2])
0.3414
>>> hh([43,36,16,2])
0.3618875544691253
>>> hh([43,36,3,2])
0.447562358276644

This suggests that Besu is 5.2x valuable toward decreasing the Herfindahl index. If we average the raw number (5.33x) and the Herfindahl contribution (5.2x), we get 5.27x.

Note that Erigon is also known to be significant inspiration for many design decisions in Reth. However, the spirit of the graph-based contribution evaluation approach is that any such indirect contributions should not be counted here, rather they should be treated as a separate edge in the graph from Erigon to Reth.

Reth has a 2% market share in Ethereum execution clients. Using Herfindahl indices (0.341 with Reth, 0.355 without it), one can argue that Reth makes the staking client distribution 3.85% more decentralized. If we take this marginal contribution as a fraction of all marginal contributions, we get (0.0385 / (0.0385 + ...)) = 0.0410, ie. Reth is doing 4% of the work of improving client decentralization. Averaging these two, we give it a 3% contribution share.

web.js is a crucial library for the user interface layer; AI estimates it to have a 20% market share.

Intuitively, it feels like execution clients are ~3x more important than the web3 library: Ethereum cannot survive without the former, though the latter is still very important.

Hence the total score is 0.2 / 0.03 * 3 = 2.22 in web3.js's favor.

Erigon and Geth are both execution clients. Their market shares are 3% and 43%.

We can evaluate their contributions to client diversity by comparing Herfindahl index of staking with and without them:

>>> def hh(l): return sum([x**2 for x in l]) / sum(l)**2
... 
>>> hh([43, 36, 16, 3, 2])
0.3414
>>> hh([43, 36, 16, 2])
0.3618875544691253
>>> hh([36, 16, 3, 2])
0.4816866728224069

This suggests Geth's contribution to decentralization is 6.8x greater than Erigon's. Meanwhile, by raw market share, Geth has 14.3x more usage. Averaging these two, we get 10.55x in Geth's favor.

I've been using Ethers.js regularly, and there's no denying it's an incredibly useful tool. Since JavaScript and TypeScript are so popular, Ethers.js has secured an important place in that ecosystem. One thing I've noticed—though it might not be unique to Ethers.js—is that the jump from version 5 to version 6 involved some major changes. I often ran into implementation issues because of dependency problems.

On another note, Reth is also very useful as a high-performance execution client built with Rust. It's important to remember that having a variety of execution clients is crucial for decentralization. Although there are alternatives similar to Ethers.js, Reth is one of the newer implementations out there, and with features that you won't find in others like ExEx, I believe it offers both uniqueness and promising potential for the future.

Very subjective here.  I have a negative historical view of Vyper.  But also fe has been in R&D forever and is currently unusable because they decided to rewrite a major core component.
